sentences.text: I thought the story might be interested-- interesting to add to others.
Our experience was a little different from others, so I thought it might, in the long run, be interesting.
Well it'll help preserve the history of what happened, and how one nation that used to be highly civilized can be corrupted to a point where they become mass murderers.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It seems impossible that a nation that produced a good and a [? solid ?] sinks-- can sink to such depths.
And that was the reason why I thought this should be preserved.
We have now been joined by my wife Hella, and by my son Frank.
Frank lives only 15 minutes by car from here, with his wife Janet and two dogs.
Yeah, well-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I don't know what to say.
[LAUGHTER]
That's right.
We-- we had a good life together all these years, many of them.
I have no regrets either.
[LAUGHTER] We had wonderful youth, and we are having wonderful years together.
We still do.
Yes.
I just feel it was a good experience.
And it was important to record these things so that they are preserved and people know later on what happened in those years.
They did, to a degree.
But I would say not-- not a huge amount, not a great deal.
Naturally, there were things ongoing as I was growing up, relating to family property and such back in Germany.
But the feeling growing up was not one of hostility and anger towards what they'd gone through, towards Germany.
I was aware of it, but it was not an overt anger.
It was-- I think the idea was to give us a feeling of security, and it was a very warm environment in which I grew up with my parents and with my father's parents.
Very, I-- I-- yeah.
Very much so.
Very much so.
Other people may have-- again, it may have been a more angry feeling towards Germany.
My father mentioned that we speak German, my brother and I.
And, you know, it's an understanding of what happened more than an emotional anger.
But it was-- it was a very secure environment growing up here.
In about four months, we're going to have a girl.
And, of course, my parents, and I think especially my father, are very pleased about that.
My father said it was the best thing in 50 years.
Of course, I'm not 50 years old so-- [LAUGHS] 49.
So I think my parents are probably as pleased as we are.
It's something we've looked forward to for some time.
No, I think we said it more or less.
And pity that our son Ralph can't be here.
He would have had a few words to say, too, I'm sure.
But he is in Washington, DC.
Thank you.
This is a picture of my grandmother, Caroline Hammelbacher, as a young girl.
This picture must have been taken around 19-- excuse me, around 1870.
It was taken in Bad Kissingen, which is a spa not too far from Nuremberg.
This is a picture of my parents and myself in Bad Kissingen, a spa [INAUDIBLE] Nuremberg.
It was taken in 1920 when I-- when I was nine years old.
On the left is my mother, Else Hammelbacher, and on the right, my father, Jacob Hammelbacher.
This is a picture of myself when I was six years old, my first day of school.
It was customary to dress the schoolboys in sailor suits and to give them a cornucopia as a gift filled with goodies.
Taken in Nuremberg.
The reason these pictures are arranged in helter skelter fashion is the fact that we did not bring any pictures with us at all.
We got them from friends and relatives and pasted them into the album in the order in which we received them, not in chronological order.
This is a picture of myself as a boy.
I would guess it was taken in 1925, or thereabouts, by a photographer in the village of [NON-ENGLISH], where we had our brewery.
This is a picture of my wife, Hella.
It must be from about 1933.
That is about the time when I fell in with love with her, and I vividly remember her in this dress.
This is one of our wedding pictures in Nuremberg in 1937.
We were all dressed up, and there's a beautiful bride.
This was the captain's dinner on the day before we arrived in the United States on the Japanese ship, [NON-ENGLISH].
The captain had trouble finding New York Harbor, but he eventually managed.
I'm in the back row center.
December 3, 1939, is right.
This was our last car in Germany, a Ford V8.
We finally had to sell it to the Nazi party for 100 marks.
The 100 marks we never got, either.
This is a picture of Hella and myself, in-- on a bench in Central Park.
We had just arrived from England and had no idea what our fate would be.
This is a picture of our oldest son Ralph Hammelbacher.
He stayed in Kenya for 10 years, a good part of the time as a safari guide, then he went into the-- into the travel wholesale business.
And this was when he was flying in Kenya in pursuit of various routes in Kenya so he could have his customers come over to Kenya and get around.
That must have been taken around-- [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] around-- around 1970.
Wasn't it October?
This is a wedding good picture of son, Frank, with Janet, nee Ingber, in September of '93, in New York.
This is my passport issued in Nuremberg in 1938, by mistake.
Without that passport, I don't know if I would ever have gotten out of Germany alive.
This is the first page of my passport showing the red J for Jude, Jew, that every German Jew had.
And every man was given the second name of Israel.
So I'm now Dr.
Hans Israel Hammelbacher.
The women were all called Sarah, in addition to their regular first name.

This is a calendar that Hella and I bring out every year, on behalf of the Blue Card, a charitable organization that takes care of Holocaust survivors in this country by making it possible for them to pay their rent and deductibles and grocery bills.
We have various themes that we think of.

This is the second in a serious called 'The Jewish Pioneers in America,' showing a picture of the statue which stands on Wacker Drive in Chicago showing George Washington in the center; the Secretary of the Treasury, Morris, on the left; and Haym Salomon on the right, who financed the revolution.
Without Haym Salomon, there would have been no money to pay for the troops that fought for the United States.
